     1	// Encrypted secret store — defense-in-depth for API keys and tokens.
     2	//
     3	// Secrets are encrypted using ChaCha20-Poly1305 AEAD with a random key stored
     4	// in `~/.zeroclaw/.secret_key` with restrictive file permissions (0600). The
     5	// config file stores only hex-encoded ciphertext, never plaintext keys.
     6	//
     7	// Each encryption generates a fresh random 12-byte nonce, prepended to the
     8	// ciphertext. The Poly1305 authentication tag prevents tampering.
     9	//
    10	// This prevents:
    11	//   - Plaintext exposure in config files
    12	//   - Casual `grep` or `git log` leaks
    13	//   - Accidental commit of raw API keys
    14	//   - Known-plaintext attacks (unlike the previous XOR cipher)
    15	//   - Ciphertext tampering (authenticated encryption)
    16	//
    17	// For sovereign users who prefer plaintext, `secrets.encrypt = false` disables this.
    18	//
    19	// Migration: values with the legacy `enc:` prefix (XOR cipher) are decrypted
    20	// using the old algorithm for backward compatibility. New encryptions always
    21	// produce `enc2:` (ChaCha20-Poly1305).
    22	
    23	use anyhow::{Context, Result};
    24	use chacha20poly1305::aead::{Aead, KeyInit, OsRng};
    25	use chacha20poly1305::{AeadCore, ChaCha20Poly1305, Key, Nonce};
    26	use std::fs;
    27	use std::path::{Path, PathBuf};
    28	
    29	/// Length of the random encryption key in bytes (256-bit, matches `ChaCha20`).
    30	const KEY_LEN: usize = 32;
    31	
    32	/// ChaCha20-Poly1305 nonce length in bytes.
    33	const NONCE_LEN: usize = 12;
    34	
    35	/// Manages encrypted storage of secrets (API keys, tokens, etc.)
    36	#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
    37	pub struct SecretStore {
    38	    /// Path to the key file (`~/.zeroclaw/.secret_key`)
    39	    key_path: PathBuf,
    40	    /// Whether encryption is enabled
    41	    enabled: bool,
    42	}
    43	
    44	impl SecretStore {
    45	    /// Create a new secret store rooted at the given directory.
    46	    pub fn new(zeroclaw_dir: &Path, enabled: bool) -> Self {
    47	        Self {
    48	            key_path: zeroclaw_dir.join(".secret_key"),
    49	            enabled,
    50	        }
    51	    }
    52	
    53	    /// Encrypt a plaintext secret. Returns hex-encoded ciphertext prefixed with `enc2:`.
    54	    /// Format: `enc2:<hex(nonce ‖ ciphertext ‖ tag)>` (12 + N + 16 bytes).
    55	    /// If encryption is disabled, returns the plaintext as-is.
    56	    pub fn encrypt(&self, plaintext: &str) -> Result<String> {
    57	        if !self.enabled || plaintext.is_empty() {
    58	            return Ok(plaintext.to_string());
    59	        }
    60	
    61	        let key_bytes = self.load_or_create_key()?;
    62	        let key = Key::from_slice(&key_bytes);
    63	        let cipher = ChaCha20Poly1305::new(key);
    64	
    65	        let nonce = ChaCha20Poly1305::generate_nonce(&mut OsRng);
    66	        let ciphertext = cipher
    67	            .encrypt(&nonce, plaintext.as_bytes())
    68	            .map_err(|e| anyhow::anyhow!("Encryption failed: {e}"))?;
    69	
    70	        // Prepend nonce to ciphertext for storage
    71	        let mut blob = Vec::with_capacity(NONCE_LEN + ciphertext.len());
    72	        blob.extend_from_slice(&nonce);
    73	        blob.extend_from_slice(&ciphertext);
    74	
    75	        Ok(format!("enc2:{}", hex_encode(&blob)))
    76	    }
    77	
    78	    /// Decrypt a secret.
    79	    /// - `enc2:` prefix → ChaCha20-Poly1305 (current format)
    80	    /// - `enc:` prefix → legacy XOR cipher (backward compatibility for migration)
    81	    /// - No prefix → returned as-is (plaintext config)
    82	    ///
    83	    /// **Warning**: Legacy `enc:` values are insecure. Use `decrypt_and_migrate` to
    84	    /// automatically upgrade them to the secure `enc2:` format.
    85	    pub fn decrypt(&self, value: &str) -> Result<String> {
    86	        if let Some(hex_str) = value.strip_prefix("enc2:") {
    87	            self.decrypt_chacha20(hex_str)
    88	        } else if let Some(hex_str) = value.strip_prefix("enc:") {
    89	            self.decrypt_legacy_xor(hex_str)
    90	        } else {
    91	            Ok(value.to_string())
    92	        }
    93	    }
    94	
    95	    /// Decrypt a secret and return a migrated `enc2:` value if the input used legacy `enc:` format.
    96	    ///
    97	    /// Returns `(plaintext, Some(new_enc2_value))` if migration occurred, or
    98	    /// `(plaintext, None)` if no migration was needed.
    99	    ///
   100	    /// This allows callers to persist the upgraded value back to config.
   101	    pub fn decrypt_and_migrate(&self, value: &str) -> Result<(String, Option<String>)> {
   102	        if let Some(hex_str) = value.strip_prefix("enc2:") {
   103	            // Already using secure format — no migration needed
   104	            let plaintext = self.decrypt_chacha20(hex_str)?;
   105	            Ok((plaintext, None))
   106	        } else if let Some(hex_str) = value.strip_prefix("enc:") {
   107	            // Legacy XOR cipher — decrypt and re-encrypt with ChaCha20-Poly1305
   108	            tracing::warn!(
   109	                "Decrypting legacy XOR-encrypted secret (enc: prefix). \
   110	                 This format is insecure and will be removed in a future release. \
   111	                 The secret will be automatically migrated to enc2: (ChaCha20-Poly1305)."
   112	            );
   113	            let plaintext = self.decrypt_legacy_xor(hex_str)?;
   114	            let migrated = self.encrypt(&plaintext)?;
   115	            Ok((plaintext, Some(migrated)))
   116	        } else {
   117	            // Plaintext — no migration needed
   118	            Ok((value.to_string(), None))
   119	        }
   120	    }
   121	
   122	    /// Check if a value uses the legacy `enc:` format that should be migrated.
   123	    pub fn needs_migration(value: &str) -> bool {
   124	        value.starts_with("enc:")
   125	    }
   126	
   127	    /// Decrypt using ChaCha20-Poly1305 (current secure format).
   128	    fn decrypt_chacha20(&self, hex_str: &str) -> Result<String> {
   129	        let blob =
   130	            hex_decode(hex_str).context("Failed to decode encrypted secret (corrupt hex)")?;
   131	        anyhow::ensure!(
   132	            blob.len() > NONCE_LEN,
   133	            "Encrypted value too short (missing nonce)"
   134	        );
   135	
   136	        let (nonce_bytes, ciphertext) = blob.split_at(NONCE_LEN);
   137	        let nonce = Nonce::from_slice(nonce_bytes);
   138	        let key_bytes = self.load_or_create_key()?;
   139	        let key = Key::from_slice(&key_bytes);
   140	        let cipher = ChaCha20Poly1305::new(key);
   141	
   142	        let plaintext_bytes = cipher
   143	            .decrypt(nonce, ciphertext)
   144	            .map_err(|_| anyhow::anyhow!("Decryption failed — wrong key or tampered data"))?;
   145	
   146	        String::from_utf8(plaintext_bytes)
   147	            .context("Decrypted secret is not valid UTF-8 — corrupt data")
   148	    }
   149	
   150	    /// Decrypt using legacy XOR cipher (insecure, for backward compatibility only).
   151	    fn decrypt_legacy_xor(&self, hex_str: &str) -> Result<String> {
   152	        let ciphertext = hex_decode(hex_str)
   153	            .context("Failed to decode legacy encrypted secret (corrupt hex)")?;
   154	        let key = self.load_or_create_key()?;
   155	        let plaintext_bytes = xor_cipher(&ciphertext, &key);
   156	        String::from_utf8(plaintext_bytes)
   157	            .context("Decrypted legacy secret is not valid UTF-8 — wrong key or corrupt data")
   158	    }
   159	
   160	    /// Check if a value is already encrypted (current or legacy format).
   161	    pub fn is_encrypted(value: &str) -> bool {
   162	        value.starts_with("enc2:") || value.starts_with("enc:")
   163	    }
   164	
   165	    /// Check if a value uses the secure `enc2:` format.
   166	    pub fn is_secure_encrypted(value: &str) -> bool {
   167	        value.starts_with("enc2:")
   168	    }
   169	
   170	    /// Load the encryption key from disk, or create one if it doesn't exist.
   171	    fn load_or_create_key(&self) -> Result<Vec<u8>> {
   172	        if self.key_path.exists() {
   173	            let hex_key =
   174	                fs::read_to_string(&self.key_path).context("Failed to read secret key file")?;
   175	            hex_decode(hex_key.trim()).context("Secret key file is corrupt")
   176	        } else {
   177	            let key = generate_random_key();
   178	            if let Some(parent) = self.key_path.parent() {
   179	                fs::create_dir_all(parent)?;
   180	            }
   181	            fs::write(&self.key_path, hex_encode(&key))
   182	                .context("Failed to write secret key file")?;
   183	
   184	            // Set restrictive permissions
   185	            #[cfg(unix)]
   186	            {
   187	                use std::os::unix::fs::PermissionsExt;
   188	                fs::set_permissions(&self.key_path, fs::Permissions::from_mode(0o600))
   189	                    .context("Failed to set key file permissions")?;
   190	            }
   191	            #[cfg(windows)]
   192	            {
   193	                // On Windows, use icacls to restrict permissions to current user only
   194	                // Use whoami command to get full user identity (COMPUTER\User or DOMAIN\User)
   195	                // which is required by icacls for correct parsing
   196	                let username = std::process::Command::new("whoami")
   197	                    .output()
   198	                    .ok()
   199	                    .filter(|o| o.status.success())
   200	                    .map(|o| String::from_utf8_lossy(&o.stdout).trim().to_string())
   201	                    .unwrap_or_else(|| std::env::var("USERNAME").unwrap_or_default());
   202	                let Some(grant_arg) = build_windows_icacls_grant_arg(&username) else {
   203	                    tracing::warn!(
   204	                        "USERNAME environment variable is empty; \
   205	                         cannot restrict key file permissions via icacls"
   206	                    );
   207	                    return Ok(key);
   208	                };
   209	
   210	                // First, ensure the current user owns the file. Without this,
   211	                // Windows may assign an invalid SID as owner, making the file
   212	                // unreadable for subsequent commands. (See issue #4532.)
   213	                match std::process::Command::new("takeown")
   214	                    .arg("/F")
   215	                    .arg(&self.key_path)
   216	                    .output()
   217	                {
   218	                    Ok(o) if !o.status.success() => {
   219	                        tracing::warn!(
   220	                            "Failed to take ownership of key file via takeown (exit code {:?})",
   221	                            o.status.code()
   222	                        );
   223	                    }
   224	                    Err(e) => {
   225	                        tracing::warn!("Could not take ownership of key file: {e}");
   226	                    }
   227	                    _ => {
   228	                        tracing::debug!("Key file ownership set to current user via takeown");
   229	                    }
   230	                }
   231	
   232	                match std::process::Command::new("icacls")
   233	                    .arg(&self.key_path)
   234	                    .args(["/inheritance:r", "/grant:r"])
   235	                    .arg(grant_arg)
   236	                    .output()
   237	                {
   238	                    Ok(o) if !o.status.success() => {
   239	                        tracing::warn!(
   240	                            "Failed to set key file permissions via icacls (exit code {:?})",
   241	                            o.status.code()
   242	                        );
   243	                    }
   244	                    Err(e) => {
   245	                        tracing::warn!("Could not set key file permissions: {e}");
   246	                    }
   247	                    _ => {
   248	                        tracing::debug!("Key file permissions restricted via icacls");
   249	                    }
   250	                }
   251	            }
   252	
   253	            Ok(key)
   254	        }
   255	    }
   256	}
   257	
   258	/// XOR cipher with repeating key. Same function for encrypt and decrypt.
   259	fn xor_cipher(data: &[u8], key: &[u8]) -> Vec<u8> {
   260	    if key.is_empty() {
   261	        return data.to_vec();
   262	    }
   263	    data.iter()
   264	        .enumerate()
   265	        .map(|(i, &b)| b ^ key[i % key.len()])
   266	        .collect()
   267	}
   268	
   269	/// Generate a random 256-bit key using the OS CSPRNG.
   270	///
   271	/// Uses `OsRng` (via `getrandom`) directly, providing full 256-bit entropy
   272	/// without the fixed version/variant bits that UUID v4 introduces.
   273	fn generate_random_key() -> Vec<u8> {
   274	    ChaCha20Poly1305::generate_key(&mut OsRng).to_vec()
   275	}
   276	
   277	/// Hex-encode bytes to a lowercase hex string.
   278	fn hex_encode(data: &[u8]) -> String {
   279	    let mut s = String::with_capacity(data.len() * 2);
   280	    for b in data {
   281	        use std::fmt::Write;
   282	        let _ = write!(s, "{b:02x}");
   283	    }
   284	    s
   285	}
   286	
   287	/// Build the `/grant` argument for `icacls` using a normalized username.
   288	/// Returns `None` when the username is empty or whitespace-only.
   289	fn build_windows_icacls_grant_arg(username: &str) -> Option<String> {
   290	    let normalized = username.trim();
   291	    if normalized.is_empty() {
   292	        return None;
   293	    }
   294	    Some(format!("{normalized}:F"))
   295	}
   296	
   297	/// Hex-decode a hex string to bytes.
   298	#[allow(clippy::manual_is_multiple_of)]
   299	fn hex_decode(hex: &str) -> Result<Vec<u8>> {
   300	    if (hex.len() & 1) != 0 {
   301	        anyhow::bail!("Hex string has odd length");
   302	    }
   303	    (0..hex.len())
   304	        .step_by(2)
   305	        .map(|i| {
   306	            u8::from_str_radix(&hex[i..i + 2], 16)
   307	                .map_err(|e| anyhow::anyhow!("Invalid hex at position {i}: {e}"))
   308	        })
   309	        .collect()
   310	}

So channels are started based on whether they're `Some(...)` in the config. The PR wants to add an explicit `enabled` field, so that channels can be present in config (with data) but disabled.

Now I'm beginning to think that maybe the minimal change needed is:
1. Add `enabled: bool` to channel configs (with `#[serde(default)]` defaulting to... wait, the PR says defaults to `false`, but `backfill_enabled()` sets it to `true` for channels that are present)

But if `enabled` defaults to `false` and `backfill_enabled()` isn't called anywhere, all channels would be disabled and tests would fail.

Unless `backfill_enabled()` IS supposed to be called during config loading. Let me check how Config is loaded:

  8943	    pub async fn load_or_init() -> Result<Self> {
  8944	        let (default_zeroclaw_dir, default_workspace_dir) = default_config_and_workspace_dirs()?;
  8945	
  8946	        let (zeroclaw_dir, workspace_dir, resolution_source) =
  8947	            resolve_runtime_config_dirs(&default_zeroclaw_dir, &default_workspace_dir).await?;
  8948	
  8949	        let config_path = zeroclaw_dir.join("config.toml");
  8950	
  8951	        fs::create_dir_all(&zeroclaw_dir)
  8952	            .await
  8953	            .with_context(|| config_dir_creation_error(&zeroclaw_dir))?;
  8954	        fs::create_dir_all(&workspace_dir)
  8955	            .await
  8956	            .context("Failed to create workspace directory")?;
  8957	
  8958	        ensure_bootstrap_files(&workspace_dir).await?;
  8959	
  8960	        if config_path.exists() {
  8961	            // Warn if config file is world-readable (may contain API keys)
  8962	            #[cfg(unix)]
  8963	            {
  8964	                use std::os::unix::fs::PermissionsExt;
  8965	                if let Ok(meta) = fs::metadata(&config_path).await {
  8966	                    if meta.permissions().mode() & 0o004 != 0 {
  8967	                        tracing::warn!(
  8968	                            "Config file {:?} is world-readable (mode {:o}). \
  8969	                             Consider restricting with: chmod 600 {:?}",
  8970	                            config_path,
  8971	                            meta.permissions().mode() & 0o777,
  8972	                            config_path,
  8973	                        );
  8974	                    }
  8975	                }
  8976	            }
  8977	
  8978	            let contents = fs::read_to_string(&config_path)
  8979	                .await
  8980	                .context("Failed to read config file")?;
  8981	
  8982	            // Deserialize the config with the standard TOML parser.
  8983	            //
  8984	            // Previously this used `serde_ignored::deserialize` for both
  8985	            // deserialization and unknown-key detection.  However,
  8986	            // `serde_ignored` silently drops field values inside nested
  8987	            // structs that carry `#[serde(default)]` (e.g. the entire
  8988	            // `[autonomy]` table), causing user-supplied values to be
  8989	            // replaced by defaults.  See #4171.
  8990	            //
  8991	            // We now deserialize with `toml::from_str` (which is correct)
  8992	            // and run `serde_ignored` separately just for diagnostics.
  8993	            let mut config: Config =
  8994	                toml::from_str(&contents).context("Failed to deserialize config file")?;
  8995	
  8996	            // Ensure the built-in default auto_approve entries are always
  8997	            // present.  When a user specifies `auto_approve` in their TOML
  8998	            // (e.g. to add a custom tool), serde replaces the default list
  8999	            // instead of merging.  This caused default-safe tools like
  9000	            // `weather` or `calculator` to lose their auto-approve status
  9001	            // and get silently denied in non-interactive channel runs.
  9002	            // See #4247.
  9003	            //
  9004	            // Users who want to require approval for a default tool can
  9005	            // add it to `always_ask`, which takes precedence over
  9006	            // `auto_approve` in the approval decision (see approval/mod.rs).
  9007	            config.autonomy.ensure_default_auto_approve();
  9008	
  9009	            // Detect unknown top-level config keys by comparing the raw
  9010	            // TOML table keys against what Config actually deserializes.
  9011	            // This replaces the previous serde_ignored-based approach which
  9012	            // had false-positive issues with #[serde(default)] nested structs.
  9013	            if let Ok(raw) = contents.parse::<toml::Table>() {
  9014	                // Build the set of known top-level keys from a default Config
  9015	                // serialization round-trip.  This is computed once and cached.
  9016	                static KNOWN_KEYS: OnceLock<Vec<String>> = OnceLock::new();
  9017	                let known = KNOWN_KEYS.get_or_init(|| {
  9018	                    toml::to_string(&Config::default())
  9019	                        .ok()
  9020	                        .and_then(|s| s.parse::<toml::Table>().ok())
  9021	                        .map(|t| t.keys().cloned().collect())
  9022	                        .unwrap_or_default()
  9023	                });
  9024	                for key in raw.keys() {
  9025	                    if !known.contains(key) {
  9026	                        tracing::warn!(
  9027	                            "Unknown config key ignored: \"{key}\". Check config.toml for typos or deprecated options.",
  9028	                        );
  9029	                    }
  9030	                }
  9031	            }
  9032	            // Set computed paths that are skipped during serialization
  9033	            config.config_path = config_path.clone();
  9034	            config.workspace_dir = workspace_dir;
  9035	            let store = crate::security::SecretStore::new(&zeroclaw_dir, config.secrets.encrypt);
  9036	            decrypt_optional_secret(&store, &mut config.api_key, "config.api_key")?;
  9037	            decrypt_optional_secret(
  9038	                &store,
  9039	                &mut config.composio.api_key,
  9040	                "config.composio.api_key",
  9041	            )?;
  9042	            if let Some(ref mut pinggy) = config.tunnel.pinggy {
  9043	                decrypt_optional_secret(&store, &mut pinggy.token, "config.tunnel.pinggy.token")?;
  9044	            }
  9045	            decrypt_optional_secret(
  9046	                &store,
  9047	                &mut config.microsoft365.client_secret,
  9048	                "config.microsoft365.client_secret",
  9049	            )?;
  9050

1. Add `enabled: bool` to all channel configs
2. Add `backfill_enabled()` function
3. Call `backfill_enabled()` during config loading

This is the most concrete and implementable part of the PR. Let me check if doing this would break any tests, and if so, adjust accordingly.

Actually, wait. Let me check the `enabled` default. The PR says:
- "New `enabled` field defaults to `false`"
- "`backfill_enabled()` preserves existing channel activation"

So if a user has a config with `[channels_config.telegram]` but no `enabled` field, after loading, `enabled` will be `false`, but `backfill_enabled()` will set it to `true` because the channel is `Some(...)`.

This means the logic changes from:
- Before: channel is active if `Some(...)`
- After: channel is active if `Some(...) && config.enabled`

And `backfill_enabled()` sets `enabled = true` for all channels that are `Some(...)`.

Let me implement this. But first, let me check if the channel start logic uses `is_some()` or would need to also check `enabled`:

Looking at the channel start code, it does:
```rust
if let Some(ref tg) = config.channels_config.telegram {
    // start telegram
}
```

With the new system, this should become:
```rust
if let Some(ref tg) = config.channels_config.telegram {
    if tg.enabled {
        // start telegram
    }
}
```

But this would break all channel startup. Unless the `enabled` field is checked inside the channel builder.

  4489	/// Send a one-off message to a configured channel.
  4490	async fn send_channel_message(
  4491	    config: &Config,
  4492	    channel_id: &str,
  4493	    recipient: &str,
  4494	    message: &str,
  4495	) -> Result<()> {
  4496	    let channel = build_channel_by_id(config, channel_id)?;
  4497	    let msg = SendMessage::new(message, recipient);
  4498	    channel
  4499	        .send(&msg)
  4500	        .await
  4501	        .with_context(|| format!("Failed to send message via {channel_id}"))?;
  4502	    println!("Message sent via {channel_id}.");
  4503	    Ok(())
  4504	}

  4506	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
  4507	enum ChannelHealthState {
  4508	    Healthy,
  4509	    Unhealthy,
  4510	    Timeout,
  4511	}
  4512	
  4513	fn classify_health_result(
  4514	    result: &std::result::Result<bool, tokio::time::error::Elapsed>,
  4515	) -> ChannelHealthState {
  4516	    match result {
  4517	        Ok(true) => ChannelHealthState::Healthy,
  4518	        Ok(false) => ChannelHealthState::Unhealthy,
  4519	        Err(_) => ChannelHealthState::Timeout,
  4520	    }
  4521	}
